problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
The only difference with E1 is the question of the problem.

Vlad built a maze out of $$$n$$$ rooms and $$$n-1$$$ bidirectional corridors. From any room $$$u$$$ any other room $$$v$$$ can be reached through a sequence of corridors. Thus, the room system forms an undirected tree.

Vlad invited $$$k$$$ friends to play a game with them.

Vlad starts the game in the room $$$1$$$ and wins if he reaches a room other than $$$1$$$, into which exactly one corridor leads. Friends are placed in the maze: the friend with number $$$i$$$ is in the room $$$x_i$$$, and no two friends are in the same room (that is, $$$x_i \neq x_j$$$ for all $$$i \neq j$$$). Friends win if one of them meets Vlad in any room or corridor before he wins.

For one unit of time, each participant of the game can go through one corridor. All participants move at the same time. Participants may not move. Each room can fit all participants at the same time.

Friends know the plan of a maze and intend to win. They don't want to waste too much energy. They ask you to determine if they can win and if they can, what minimum number of friends must remain in the maze so that they can always catch Vlad.

In other words, you need to determine the size of the minimum (by the number of elements) subset of friends who can catch Vlad or say that such a subset does not exist.

Time limit: 2s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line of the input contains an integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$) — the number of test cases in the input. The input contains an empty string before each test case.

The first line of the test case contains two numbers $$$n$$$ and $$$k$$$ ($$$1 \le k < n \le 2\cdot 10^5$$$) — the number of rooms and friends, respectively.

The next line of the test case contains $$$k$$$ integers $$$x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k$$$ ($$$2 \le x_i \le n$$$) — numbers of rooms with friends. All $$$x_i$$$ are different.

The next $$$n-1$$$ lines contain descriptions of the corridors, two numbers per line $$$v_j$$$ and $$$u_j$$$ ($$$1 \le u_j, v_j \le n$$$) — numbers of rooms that connect the $$$j$$$ corridor. All corridors are bidirectional. From any room, you can go to any other by moving along the corridors.

It is guaranteed that the sum of the values $$$n$$$ over all test cases in the test is not greater than $$$2\cdot10^5$$$.

Output:
Print $$$t$$$ lines, each line containing the answer to the corresponding test case. The answer to a test case should be $$$-1$$$ if Vlad wins anyway and a minimal number of friends otherwise.

Note:
In the first set of inputs, even if all the friends stay in the maze, Vlad can still win. Therefore, the answer is "-1".

In the second set of inputs it is enough to leave friends from rooms $$$6$$$ and $$$7$$$. Then Vlad will not be able to win. The answer is "2".

In the third and fourth sets of inputs Vlad cannot win only if all his friends stay in the maze. Therefore the answers are "1" and "2".
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    4
    
    8 2
    5 3
    4 7
    2 5
    1 6
    3 6
    7 2
    1 7
    6 8
    
    8 4
    6 5 7 3
    4 7
    2 5
    1 6
    3 6
    7 2
    1 7
    6 8
    
    3 1
    2
    1 2
    2 3
    
    3 2
    2 3
    3 1
    1 2
    Output:
    -1
    2
    1
    2

<code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

void solve() {
    string a;
    getline(cin, a);
    ll n, k;
    cin >> n >> k;
    vll x(k);
    for (ll i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
        cin >> x[i];
        --x[i];
    }
    vector<vll> M(n);
    for (ll i = 0; i < n - 1; ++i) {
        ll p, q;
        cin >> p >> q;
        --p; --q;
        M[p].pb(q);
        M[q].pb(p);
    }
    vll F(n, -1);
    queue<ll> q;
    unordered_set<ll> S(all(x));
    for (ll j : S) {
        F[j] = 0;
        q.push(j);
    }
    while (!q.empty()) {
        ll u = q.front(); q.pop();
        for (ll v : M[u]) {
            if (F[v] == -1) {
                F[v] = F[u] + 1;
                q.push(v);
            }
        }
    }
    vll V(n, -1);
    queue<ll> q2;
    V[0] = 0;
    q2.push(0);
    ll ret = 0;
    while (!q2.empty()) {
        ll u = q2.front(); q2.pop();
        for (ll v : M[u]) {
            if (V[v] == -1) {
                if (F[v] > V[u] + 1) {
                    V[v] = V[u] + 1;
                    q2.push(v);
                } else {
                    ++ret;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    vll M1;
    for (ll i = 1; i < n; ++i) {
        if (sz(M[i]) == 1) {
            M1.pb(i);
        }
    }
    bool bol = false;
    for (ll i : M1) {
        if (F[i] > V[i] && V[i] != -1) {
            bol = true;
            break;
        }
    }
    if (bol) {
        cout << -1 << endl;
    } else {
        cout << ret << endl;
    }
}

int main() {
    fastio;
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    cin.ignore();
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
</code>

Now the simulation approach: for a fixed subset, I simulate Vlad's movement toward a chosen leaf, friends move toward Vlad. But Vlad can choose any leaf and any tie-breaking. To check if a subset always catches Vlad, I'd need to simulate all possible Vlad strategies — which is essentially a game tree. The number of leaves could be O(n), and Vlad's choices branch. Simulating all paths for all subsets is exponential.

The hypothesis says "binary search on m, for fixed m select subset uniformly at random or via heuristic, simulate, repeat many times." But random sampling doesn't guarantee finding a subset that always catches Vlad — it might miss the right subset. And even for a fixed subset, simulating one Vlad path doesn't prove Vlad can't escape via a different path. I'd need to check all Vlad strategies against that subset. That's a min-max game: Vlad chooses path to minimize chance of being caught, friends move optimally. This is not a simple simulation — it's a full game tree search.

For n up to 2×10^5, the tree is large. The number of leaves can be O(n). For a fixed subset of friends, checking if Vlad can escape requires computing for each leaf whether Vlad can reach it before any friend intercepts him. That's a deterministic property: Vlad can reach leaf L if for every friend f, dist(1, L) < dist(f, L) (since friends move toward Vlad, but actually friends can intercept on the path — the condition is more subtle: a friend can intercept if dist(f, v) ≤ dist(1, v) for some node v on Vlad's path to L). This is a known property from the "escape the maze" type problems — it's about comparing distances from root and from friends to each node.

The simulation with random subsets and random Vlad choices is a heuristic that might approximate the answer but doesn't guarantee correctness. For a competitive programming problem, we need exact answer. The random simulation would need to be run many times and still might miss a counterexample. The time complexity: binary search over m (log k ≈ 17), for each m we'd need to sample subsets and simulate. If we sample 1000 subsets and simulate Vlad's path (O(n) per simulation), that's 17 × 1000 × 2×10^5 ≈ 3.4×10^9 operations, way over budget.

wait — the simulation per subset requires checking all Vlad strategies, not just one random path. To verify a subset works, I'd need to check all leaves, which is O(n) per subset. Binary search × subsets sampled × O(n) is infeasible. And random sampling doesn't guarantee finding the minimal m.

LEARNING: Random simulation can't verify a subset catches Vlad for all his strategies; checking all leaves is O(n) per subset, and binary search × sampling × O(n) = 3.4×10^9 ops, 17× over budget.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem, we need to analyze the structure of the maze and the positions of the friends. The key observation is that Vlad wins if he can reach a leaf node (a node with exactly one outgoing corridor) other than room 1. We need to determine if there exists a subset of friends that can prevent Vlad from reaching such a leaf node. First, let's understand the graph structure: - The maze forms a tree with \( n \) nodes and \( n-1 \) edges. - A leaf node is a node with degree 1 (except for room 1, which is special). We need to find the smallest subset of friends that can block all paths to leaf nodes except possibly room 1. This means that if a friend is placed in a node, that node becomes unavailable to Vlad's path. ### Step-by-Step Approach: 1. **Identify Leaf Nodes**: Traverse the tree to identify all leaf nodes (nodes with degree 1 except room 1). 2. **Block Paths**: For each leaf node, simulate removing it and checking if Vlad can still reach another leaf node (other than room 1). 3. **Subset Selection**: Determine the minimum subset of friends needed to block all critical paths. ### Detailed Steps: 1. **Tree Traversal**: - Build the adjacency list representation of the tree. - Perform a DFS to count degrees and collect leaf nodes (excluding room 1). 2. **Simulate Removal**: - For each leaf node, temporarily remove it from the tree. - Perform BFS/DFS starting from room 1 to see if any other leaf node is reachable. - If no other leaf node is reachable, then blocking that leaf node is sufficient. 3. **Greedy Subset Selection**: - Use a greedy algorithm to select the smallest subset of friends that cover all critical leaf nodes. - Each selected friend covers one leaf node or multiple leaf nodes if they share a common ancestor. ### Implementation Details: - Use a priority queue to always pick the leaf node that has the maximum depth from room 1 to prioritize covering deeper nodes. - Track visited nodes to avoid revisiting. ### Complexity Analysis: - Building adjacency list: \( O(n + (n-1)) = O(n) \) - Tree traversal: \( O(n) \) - Simulating removal for each leaf node: \( O(n) \) per simulation - Greedy selection: \( O(k) \) Total complexity: \( O(t \times n \log n) \), where \( t \) is the number of test cases, and \( n \) is the total number of nodes across all test cases. This approach ensures we efficiently find the minimum subset of friends needed to block Vlad's path to any leaf node other than room 1. #include #include #include #include #include using namespace std; const int MAXN = 2 * 100005; int n, k; int parent[MAXN]; vector adj[MAXN]; void dfs(int u, int p) { parent[u] = p; for (int v : adj[u]) { if (v != p) dfs(v, u); } } bool bfs(const vector& leaves, unordered_set& covered, int start) { queue q; q.push(start); while (!q.empty()) { int u = q.front(); q.pop(); if (covered.count(u)) continue; covered.insert(u); for (int v : adj[u]) { if (covered.count(v)) continue; if (leaves.count(v)) return true; q.push(v); } } return false; } int min_friends_to_catch_vlad(const vector& friends, const vector& leaves) { unordered_set covered; for (int f : friends) { if (!bfs(leaves, covered, f)) return 1; } return -1; } int main() { ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) { cin >> n >> k; for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) adj[i].clear(); for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; ++i) { int u, v; cin >> u >> v; adj[u].push_back(v); adj[v].push_back(u); } vector friends(k); for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) cin >> friends[i]; // Find all leaf nodes (degree 1 except room 1) vector leaves; for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) { if (adj[i].size() == 1 && i != 1) leaves.push_back(i); } // Check if Vlad can win bool vlad_can_win = bfs(leaves, {}, 1); if (vlad_can_win) { cout << -1 << endl; continue; } // Find minimum number of friends to block Vlad int result = min_friends_to_catch_vlad(friends, leaves); cout << result << endl; } return 0; }
finetuned.think:
To solve this problem, we need to analyze the structure of the maze and the positions of the friends. The key observation is that Vlad wins if he can reach a leaf node (a node with exactly one outgoing corridor) other than room 1. We need to determine if there exists a subset of friends that can prevent Vlad from reaching such a leaf node.

First, let's understand the graph structure:
- The maze forms a tree with \( n \) nodes and \( n-1 \) edges.
- A leaf node is a node with degree 1 (except for room 1, which is special).

We need to find the smallest subset of friends that can block all paths to leaf nodes except possibly room 1. This means that if a friend is placed in a node, that node becomes unavailable to Vlad's path.

### Step-by-Step Approach:

1. **Identify Leaf Nodes**: Traverse the tree to identify all leaf nodes (nodes with degree 1 except room 1).
2. **Block Paths**: For each leaf node, simulate removing it and checking if Vlad can still reach another leaf node (other than room 1).
3. **Subset Selection**: Determine the minimum subset of friends needed to block all critical paths.

### Detailed Steps:

1. **Tree Traversal**:
   - Build the adjacency list representation of the tree.
   - Perform a DFS to count degrees and collect leaf nodes (excluding room 1).

2. **Simulate Removal**:
   - For each leaf node, temporarily remove it from the tree.
   - Perform BFS/DFS starting from room 1 to see if any other leaf node is reachable.
   - If no other leaf node is reachable, then blocking that leaf node is sufficient.

3. **Greedy Subset Selection**:
   - Use a greedy algorithm to select the smallest subset of friends that cover all critical leaf nodes.
   - Each selected friend covers one leaf node or multiple leaf nodes if they share a common ancestor.

### Implementation Details:

- Use a priority queue to always pick the leaf node that has the maximum depth from room 1 to prioritize covering deeper nodes.
- Track visited nodes to avoid revisiting.

### Complexity Analysis:

- Building adjacency list: \( O(n + (n-1)) = O(n) \)
- Tree traversal: \( O(n) \)
- Simulating removal for each leaf node: \( O(n) \) per simulation
- Greedy selection: \( O(k) \)

Total complexity: \( O(t \times n \log n) \), where \( t \) is the number of test cases, and \( n \) is the total number of nodes across all test cases.

This approach ensures we efficiently find the minimum subset of friends needed to block Vlad's path to any leaf node other than room 1.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
This year Alex has finished school, and now he is a first-year student of Berland State University. For him it was a total surprise that even though he studies programming, he still has to attend physical education lessons. The end of the term is very soon, but, unfortunately, Alex still hasn't attended a single lesson!

Since Alex doesn't want to get expelled, he wants to know the number of working days left until the end of the term, so he can attend physical education lessons during these days. But in BSU calculating the number of working days is a complicated matter:

There are n days left before the end of the term (numbered from 1 to n), and initially all of them are working days. Then the university staff sequentially publishes q orders, one after another. Each order is characterised by three numbers l, r and k:

- If k = 1, then all days from l to r (inclusive) become non-working days. If some of these days are made working days by some previous order, then these days still become non-working days;
- If k = 2, then all days from l to r (inclusive) become working days. If some of these days are made non-working days by some previous order, then these days still become working days.

Help Alex to determine the number of working days left after each order!

Time limit: 1s
Memory limit: 256MB

Input:
The first line contains one integer n, and the second line — one integer q (1 ≤ n ≤ 109, 1 ≤ q ≤ 3·105) — the number of days left before the end of the term, and the number of orders, respectively.

Then q lines follow, i-th line containing three integers li, ri and ki representing i-th order (1 ≤ li ≤ ri ≤ n, 1 ≤ ki ≤ 2).

Output:
Print q integers. i-th of them must be equal to the number of working days left until the end of the term after the first i orders are published.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    4
    6
    1 2 1
    3 4 1
    2 3 2
    1 3 2
    2 4 1
    1 4 2
    Output:
    2
    0
    2
    3
    1
    4

The hypothesis is coordinate compression: collect all l and r from queries, plus 1 and n, sort them, and each compressed index represents a block of consecutive original days. Then we maintain a boolean array over these compressed blocks. Let me set this up concretely. I'll collect all unique endpoints: for each query (l, r, k), I add l and r+1 (or just l and r, but I need to handle ranges properly). Actually, the standard way: collect all l and r+1 to mark interval boundaries, plus 1 and n+1. Then sort and deduplicate. Each compressed index i corresponds to original days from compressed[i] to compressed[i+1]-1. The number of blocks is at most 2q+2, so about 6×10^5 blocks. That's manageable. For each query (l, r, k), I find the compressed index L where compressed[L] = l, and R where compressed[R] = r+1. Then I iterate i from L to R-1 and set block_status[i] = (k == 2) — true means working, false means non-working. After each query, I sum over all blocks: if block_status[i] is true, add (compressed[i+1] - compressed[i]) to the total working days. Let me trace Example 1. n=4, q=6. Queries: 1) 1 2 1 → make days 1-2 non-working 2) 3 4 1 → make days 3-4 non-working 3) 2 3 2 → make days 2-3 working 4) 1 3 2 → make days 1-3 working 5) 2 4 1 → make days 2-4 non-working 6) 1 4 2 → make days 1-4 working First, collect endpoints. I'll use l and r+1 for each query, plus 1 and n+1=5. Query 1: l=1, r+1=3 Query 2: l=3, r+1=5 Query 3: l=2, r+1=4 Query 4: l=1, r+1=4 Query 5: l=2, r+1=5 Query 6: l=1, r+1=5 Plus 1 and 5. All collected: 1, 3, 3, 5, 2, 4, 1, 4, 2, 5, 1, 5, 1, 5. Unique sorted: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. So compressed points: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Blocks: block 0: days 1 to 1 (length 1) block 1: days 2 to 2 (length 1) block 2: days 3 to 3 (length 1) block 3: days 4 to 4 (length 1) Total 4 blocks. Initially all working: status = [true, true, true, true]. Total = 4. Query 1: 1 2 1. l=1, r=2. Find L: compressed[L]=1 → L=0. r+1=3, compressed[R]=3 → R=2. Iterate i=0 to 1: set status[0]=false, status[1]=false. Status: [false, false, true, true]. Sum: block 0 length 0 + block 1 length 0 + block 2 length 1 + block 3 length 1 = 2. Output 2. Matches example. Query 2: 3 4 1. l=3 → L=2. r+1=5 → R=4. i=2,3: status[2]=false, status[3]=false. Status: [false, false, false, false]. Sum = 0. Output 0. Matches. Query 3: 2 3 2. l=2 → L=1. r+1=4 → R=3. i=1,2: status[1]=true, status[2]=true. Status: [false, true, true, false]. Sum: block 1 length 1 + block 2 length 1 = 2. Output 2. Matches. Query 4: 1 3 2. l=1 → L=0. r+1=4 → R=3. i=0,1,2: status[0]=true, status[1]=true, status[2]=true. Status: [true, true, true, false]. Sum: 1+1+1+0 = 3. Output 3. Matches. Query 5: 2 4 1. l=2 → L=1. r+1=5 → R=4. i=1,2,3: status[1]=false, status[2]=false, status[3]=false. Status: [true, false, false, false]. Sum: 1+0+0+0 = 1. Output 1. Matches. Query 6: 1 4 2. l=1 → L=0. r+1=5 → R=4. i=0,1,2,3: all true. Status: [true, true, true, true]. Sum = 4. Output 4. Matches. So the approach produces correct output for Example 1. Now constraints: n ≤ 10^9, q ≤ 3×10^5. The number of unique endpoints is at most 2q + 2 = 600,002. So the compressed array has at most ~600k blocks. Each query does a range update over some number of blocks. In the worst case, a query could span all blocks — that's O(number of blocks) per query. With q=3×10^5 and blocks≈6×10^5, q × blocks = 3×10^5 × 6×10^5 = 1.8×10^11 operations. Time limit is 1 second, budget ≈ 10^8 ops. That's 1800× over budget. For instance, alternating between setting the whole range to working and non-working. Each such query touches all ~600k blocks. 3×10^5 queries × 6×10^5 blocks = 1.8×10^11 iterations. oh wait — each query iterates over O(blocks) in the worst case, giving q × blocks = 1.8×10^11 ops, 1800× over the 10^8 budget. LEARNING: Naive iteration over compressed blocks per query is O(q × number_of_blocks) = 1.8×10^11 ops, 1800× over the 10^8 budget. I'll maintain a sorted list of all unique coordinate points that appear as l or r+1 in any order. For each order (l, r, k), I add l and r+1 to the set of points. LEARNING: Rebuilding prefix sums over all O(q) blocks after each order costs O(q²)=4.5×10^10 ops, 450× over the 10^8 budget. Right, so nothing worked so far. The naive simulation is impossible with n up to 1e9, and every compressed approach blew up because I kept reprocessing the entire compressed timeline after each query, which is O(q²). The core issue is that each order is a range assignment that can flip large contiguous blocks of days, and I need to reflect that change in my representation of working days without scanning everything from scratch. Hmm... The nagging thread is that the working days are just n minus the total length of all non-working intervals. And if I could maintain a set of disjoint intervals representing non-working days, each update would only affect intervals overlapping the [l, r] range. A balanced binary search tree keyed by interval start, like a std::map in C++, could do that. Each operation would be O(log m) where m is the number of intervals, and m is O(q) worst-case. Actually a=2 not < l=1, so no left. Right part? b=4 not > r=4, so no right. No parts added. No new interval for k=2. Net change -3. total_non_working: 3 -> 0. Working = 4. Output 4. Matches the example output: 2,0,2,3,1,4. aha! — the key is that each update only touches O(number of overlapping intervals) intervals, and because each interval is added or removed at most once, the total number of interval operations across all q is O(q). The balanced tree keeps each operation O(log q), so total O(q log q), which fits. The subtle part is correctly finding all overlapping intervals by checking the one before lower_bound, and handling splits to preserve disjointness. finetuned.full:
<think>
To solve this problem efficiently given the large constraints (n up to \(10^9\) and q up to \(3 \times 10^5\)), we need a data structure that supports range updates and point queries. A segment tree or Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree) can handle this, but they typically support point updates and range queries or vice versa. Since we need both types of operations, we will use a segment tree with lazy propagation.

### Steps to Solve:

1. **Segment Tree Initialization**:
   - We need a segment tree where each node stores the count of working days in its interval.
   - Initially, all intervals have working days, so the initial value at each node should be `r - l + 1`.

2. **Lazy Propagation**:
   - We maintain a lazy array for pending updates.
   - When updating a range `[l, r]` with `k = 1`, we mark the interval as "non-working" but keep the count unchanged.
   - When updating a range `[l, r]` with `k = 2`, we mark the interval as "working" but keep the count unchanged.
   - When querying the number of working days, we apply the lazy changes on the fly.

3. **Handling Overlapping Updates**:
   - Lazy propagation ensures that when multiple updates overlap, they are applied correctly. If two updates conflict (e.g., one makes it non-working and another makes it working), they would cancel each other out because the count remains the same.

4. **Efficiency**:
   - Segment trees with lazy propagation allow us to update a range in \(O(\log n)\) and query a point in \(O(\log n)\).
   - Given \(q \leq 3 \times 10^5\), this complexity is sufficient within the time limit.

### Implementation Details:

- Define a segment tree node that stores the count of working days and a lazy flag indicating whether the interval is marked as non-working or working.
- Implement the `push_down` function to propagate lazy values to children nodes.
- Implement the `update_range` function to handle the range updates with lazy propagation.
- Implement the `query_point` function to answer the number of working days at a specific day.

### Sample Code Outline:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>

using namespace std;

struct Node {
    int start, end, sum;
    bool lazy;
};

const int MAXN = 1000005; // Adjust based on actual maximum size needed
Node seg[MAXN * 4];

void build(int index, int start, int end) {
    seg[index].start = start;
    seg[index].end = end;
    if (start == end) {
        seg[index].sum = 1; // Assuming 1-based indexing for simplicity
        return;
    }
    int mid = (start + end) / 2;
    build(index * 2, start, mid);
    build(index * 2 + 1, mid + 1, end);
    seg[index].sum = seg[index * 2].sum + seg[index * 2 + 1].sum;
}

void push_down(int index) {
    if (seg[index].lazy) {
        int mid = (seg[index].start + seg[index].end) / 2;
        if (seg[index].lazy == 1) { // Non-working
            seg[index * 2].sum = mid - seg[index * 2].start + 1;
            seg[index * 2].lazy = 1;
            seg[index * 2 + 1].sum = seg[index * 2 + 1].end - mid;
            seg[index * 2 + 1].lazy = 1;
        } else { // Working
            seg[index * 2].sum = 0;
            seg[index * 2].lazy = 2;
            seg[index * 2 + 1].sum = 0;
            seg[index * 2 + 1].lazy = 2;
        }
        seg[index].lazy = 0;
    }
}

void update_range(int index, int start, int end, int k) {
    if (seg[index].start > end || seg[index].end < start)
        return;
    if (seg[index].start >= start && seg[index].end <= end) {
        if (k == 1) {
            seg[index].sum = seg[index].end - seg[index].start + 1;
        } else {
            seg[index].sum = 0;
        }
        seg[index].lazy = k;
        return;
    }
    push_down(index);
    int mid = (seg[index].start + seg[index].end) / 2;
    update_range(index * 2, start, end, k);
    update_range(index * 2 + 1, start, end, k);
    seg[index].sum = seg[index * 2].sum + seg[index * 2 + 1].sum;
}

int query_point(int index, int pos) {
    if (seg[index].start == seg[index].end)
        return seg[index].sum;
    push_down(index);
    int mid = (seg[index].start + seg[index].end) / 2;
    if (pos <= mid)
        return query_point(index * 2, pos);
    else
        return query_point(index * 2 + 1, pos);
}

int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n, q;
    cin >> n >> q;
    build(1, 1, n);

    vector<int> results(q);
    for (int i = 0; i < q; ++i) {
        int l, r, k;
        cin >> l >> r >> k;
        update_range(1, l, r, k);
        results[i] = query_point(1, 1); // Querying for day 1
    }

    for (int result : results)
        cout << result << '\n';

    return 0;
}
```

### Final Check:

- Ensure the segment tree handles overlapping ranges correctly.
- Verify that the lazy propagation logic works as expected for both types of updates.
- Confirm that the output matches the examples provided.

With these steps and implementation details, we should be able to handle the constraints efficiently and produce correct results.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
Juan has a beautiful tree with $$$n$$$ nodes numbered from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$. There are also $$$k$$$ distinct colors numbered from $$$1$$$ to $$$k$$$. Each node $$$u$$$ in the tree has its own set $$$C_u$$$ which contains colors. Let's denote with $$$s$$$ the quantity $$$\sum_{i=1}^n \left| C_i \right|$$$.

There are $$$q$$$ queries where you will be given nodes $$$u$$$ and $$$v$$$. Let $$$P$$$ denote the set of all nodes in the simple path between $$$u$$$ and $$$v$$$, inclusive. You are asked to calculate for each query the following quantity: $$$$$$\left| \bigcap_{w \in P} C_w \right|$$$$$$

That is, the cardinality of the intersection of the sets of all the nodes in the path from $$$u$$$ to $$$v$$$. In other words, calculate how many colors appear in every set on the path from $$$u$$$ to $$$v$$$.

Input:
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains four integers $$$n$$$, $$$k$$$, $$$s$$$, and $$$q$$$ ($$$1 \le n, k, q \le 3 \cdot 10^5, 1 \le s \le \min (nk, 3 \cdot 10^5)$$$) — the number of nodes in the tree, the number of distinct colors, the sum of the cardinalities of all sets of colors, and the number of queries, respectively.

The next $$$n-1$$$ lines each contain two integers $$$u$$$, $$$v$$$ ($$$1 \le u, v \le n$$$), indicating that there's an edge between nodes $$$u$$$ and $$$v$$$.

The next $$$s$$$ lines each contain two integers $$$v$$$ and $$$x$$$ ($$$1 \le v \le n, 1 \le x \le k$$$) indicating that color $$$x$$$ is in $$$C_v$$$. All $$$s$$$ lines are pairwise distinct.

The next $$$q$$$ lines each contain two integers $$$u$$$ and $$$v$$$ ($$$1 \le u, v \le n$$$), indicating a query between nodes $$$u$$$ and $$$v$$$.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$3 \cdot 10^5$$$.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$k$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$3 \cdot 10^5$$$.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$s$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$3 \cdot 10^5$$$.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$q$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$3 \cdot 10^5$$$.

Output:
For each test case, output a line: the answer to all queries in the order they appear in the input, separated by spaces.
Examples:
N/A

C_2 = {1,2}
C_3 = {1,2}
C_4 = {1}
C_5 = {2}
So s=1+2+2+1+1=7.

Query: u=4, v=5. Path: 4-3-2-5. Nodes on path: {4,3,2,5}. Intersection of their color sets:
C_4={1}, C_3={1,2}, C_2={1,2}, C_5={2}. Intersection = {1}∩{1,2}∩{1,2}∩{2} = ∅. Answer should be 0.

Now my approach: bitsets for each color:
color 1: nodes {1,2,3,4} → bitset[1]=1, [2]=1, [3]=1, [4]=1, [5]=0
color 2: nodes {2,3,5} → bitset[1]=0, [2]=1, [3]=1, [4]=0, [5]=1
color 3: nodes {} → all 0.

Query u=4, v=5. LCA of 4 and 5 is 2. Path nodes: from 4 up to 2: 4,3,2. From 5 up to 2: 5,2. Union: {2,3,4,5}. I iterate colors in C_4 = {1}. Check color 1: test bitset[1] at nodes 4,3,2,5. bitset[1][4]=1, [3]=1, [2]=1, [5]=0 → fails at node 5. So color 1 not counted. Answer 0. Correct.

Another query: u=1, v=3. Path: 1-2-3. Nodes {1,2,3}. Colors in C_1={1}. Check color 1: bitset[1][1]=1, [2]=1, [3]=1 → all pass. Count 1. Intersection of C_1={1}, C_2={1,2}, C_3={1,2} is {1}. Correct.

Now the complexity. For each query, I need the path nodes. Walking up from u to LCA and v to LCA gives O(path length) nodes. Path length can be O(n) in worst case (a line). For each query, I iterate colors in C_u. The number of colors in C_u can be up to s, which is O(3·10^5) total but per node could be O(s) in worst case if one node holds all colors. For each such color, I test O(path length) bits. So per query worst case: O(|C_u| × path_length). In a line tree, path_length = O(n), |C_u| could be O(s). So per query O(n·s) worst case. With q up to 3·10^5, total worst case O(q·n·s) which is astronomically large.

But wait — the constraints say sum of s over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5, and sum of n ≤ 3·10^5, sum of q ≤ 3·10^5. The product n·s could be (3·10^5)×(3·10^5) = 9·10^10 if concentrated in one test case. Per query that's 9·10^10 operations. With q=3·10^5, total 2.7·10^16. Budget is maybe 2·10^8. That's 10^8× over.

But I'm not actually iterating all path nodes for each color — I said I'd use bitwise AND on chunks. The idea is: for a path, instead of checking each node individually, I could precompute something like for each color, a bitset of size n, and for a path I need to check if all nodes in the path have the bit set. That's equivalent to: the AND of bitsets of all nodes on the path, tested for that color. But I don't have bitsets per node — I have bitsets per color. To check if color c is in all path nodes, I need to verify that for every node w on the path, bitset_c[w] = 1. That's exactly checking if the path is a subset of the set of nodes containing color c. So I need to test if the path nodes are all contained in the color's node set. This is a reachability/ancestry condition: the nodes containing color c form some subset of the tree. The path is contained in that subset iff the path doesn't cross outside. But checking that efficiently per query per color still seems hard.

The core issue: I'm doing per-color work that could be O(path_length) per color per query. Even with bitset chunking, the number of chunks is n/64 ≈ 5·10^3 for n=3·10^5. Per color per query, I'd AND together the chunks corresponding to path nodes. But I still need to gather the path nodes' bitsets — wait, I don't have per-node bitsets, I have per-color bitsets. To AND them, I'd need to extract the per-node bitsets for all nodes on the path, which means for each node on the path, I need its color membership vector (size k bits). That's k bits per node. If I store per-node bitsets of size k, total memory is n×k bits = 9·10^10 bits ≈ 11 GB, infeasible. And per query, I'd need to AND O(path_length) such bitsets, each of size k/64 words. Path length O(n), k up to 3·10^5, so per query O(n·k/64) ≈ 1.4·10^9 operations. Too slow.

oh wait — the per-color bitset approach fundamentally requires either iterating all path nodes per color or storing per-node bitsets which blows up memory. The product of path length and number of colors checked per query is the bottleneck.

LEARNING: Per-color bitset check on path nodes costs O(|C_u| × path_length) per query; with n=3·10^5, s=3·10^5, worst-case per query is 9·10^10 operations, 450× over budget even for a single query.

I'll build an HLD over the tree, then a segment tree over the Euler tour order. Each leaf in the segment tree corresponds to one node and stores a frequency array of size k — for each color, how many times it appears in that node's set C_u. Internal segment tree nodes aggregate by summing the frequency arrays of their children. For a query (u, v), I decompose the path into O(log n) HLD segments. For each segment [l, r] in the Euler order, I query the segment tree to get a summed frequency array for that segment. I maintain a global array min_freq[1..k] initialized to infinity. For each segment's frequency array, I do min_freq[c] = min(min_freq[c], seg_freq[c]) for all c. After processing all O(log n) segments, the answer is the count of colors c where min_freq[c] equals the total number of nodes on the path (which is depth[u] + depth[v] - 2*depth[lca] + 1). Colors that never appear in any segment will stay at infinity and won't match.

Let me trace this on a concrete small example since no example is given in the problem. I'll construct one: n=5, tree edges: 1-2, 2-3, 2-4, 4-5. k=3 colors. Color sets: C_1={1}, C_2={1,2}, C_3={1,2}, C_4={1,3}, C_5={1,2,3}. So s = 1+2+2+2+3 = 10. Query: u=3, v=5. Path: 3-2-4-5. Nodes on path: {3,2,4,5}. Intersection of their color sets: C_3 ∩ C_2 ∩ C_4 ∩ C_5 = {1,2} ∩ {1,2} ∩ {1,3} ∩ {1,2,3} = {1}. Answer should be 1.

Now HLD: root at 1. Heavy child of 1 is 2 (subtree sizes: 2 has 4 nodes, others 0). Heavy child of 2: say 4 (subtree 4 has 2 nodes, 3 has 1). Heavy child of 4: 5. Euler order by HLD (dfs heavy first): 1, 2, 4, 5, 3. Let's assign positions: pos[1]=1, pos[2]=2, pos[4]=3, pos[5]=4, pos[3]=5.

Segment tree leaves store frequency arrays of size k=3:
leaf 1 (node 1): [1,0,0]  (color 1 once)
leaf 2 (node 2): [1,1,0]
leaf 3 (node 4): [1,0,1]
leaf 4 (node 5): [1,1,1]
leaf 5 (node 3): [1,1,0]

Internal nodes sum these. Root covers [1,5] sums to [5,3,2].

Query u=3, v=5. Path decomposition: find LCA(3,5)=2. Path from 3 up to 2: node 3 only. Path from 5 up to 2: nodes 5,4. HLD segments: node 3 is at pos 5, segment [5,5]. Nodes 5 and 4: if they're in same heavy chain? 5 is heavy child of 4, 4 is heavy child of 2. So 5 and 4 are in chain starting at 2? Actually heavy chain from 2 goes 2-4-5. So segment for 5 up to 2 excluding 2: positions of 5 and 4 are [3,4] (pos 3=4, pos 4=5). So segments: [5,5] and [3,4].

Query segment tree for [5,5]: leaf 5 → [1,1,0].
min_freq initialized to INF. After [5,5]: min_freq = [1,1,0].

Query [3,4]: internal node covering leaves 3 and 4 → leaf3 [1,0,1] + leaf4 [1,1,1] = [2,1,2].
Update min_freq: min(1,2)=1, min(1,1)=1, min(0,2)=0 → min_freq = [1,1,0].

Path length = depth[3]+depth[5]-2*depth[2]+1 = 2+3-2*1+1 = 4. Nodes on path: 3,2,4,5 (4 nodes). Check which colors have min_freq = 4? min_freq = [1,1,0], none equal 4. But answer should be 1 (color 1 appears in all 4 nodes). The problem: min_freq tracks minimum frequency across segments, but what we need is: for each color, does it appear in every node on the path? The segment tree sums frequencies within each segment, but a color could appear in some nodes of a segment and not others, and the sum doesn't tell us that.

For example, segment [3,4] covers nodes 4 and 5. Color 1 appears in both (freq 1 each, sum=2). Color 2 appears in node 5 only (sum=1). Color 3 appears in both (sum=2). But min_freq across segments doesn't capture per-node presence. We'd need to know, for each color, the count of nodes in the segment that have that color — not the sum of frequencies (since a node could have a color multiple times? Actually sets have distinct colors, so frequency is 0 or 1 per node). So the sum in a segment equals the number of nodes in that segment that contain the color. For a segment of length L, if the sum for color c equals L, then color c appears in every node of that segment. Then across all segments, if the sum equals the segment length for every segment, the color appears in all nodes on the path.

So the correct check: for each segment of length len, the segment tree returns sum_freq[c]. If sum_freq[c] == len for that segment, then color c is present in all nodes of that segment. We need this to hold for all segments. So we could track a boolean array: initially all true. For each segment, for each color c, if sum_freq[c] < len, mark it false. At the end, count colors still true. But iterating over k colors per segment gives O(k log n) per query. With k up to 3×10^5 and q up to 3×10^5, that's 9×10^10 operations, same order as the bitset approach already rejected.

The pruning idea — skip rectangles where area exceeds best answer — doesn't help because the bottleneck is per-query processing of k colors across O(log n) segments, not the enumeration of rectangles.

The fundamental issue: any approach that iterates over all k colors per query will be O(k log n) per query, which with k,q ≤ 3×10^5 is ~10^10–10^11 operations, far beyond budget. The problem requires a way to avoid scanning all colors per query, perhaps by only considering colors that actually appear on the path, or using some meet-in-the-middle or DSU technique as suggested by the tags.

LEARNING: The segment-tree-of-frequency-arrays approach requires O(k log n) per query to check all colors across segments; with k,q ≤ 3·10^5 this is ~10^10 operations, exceeding any reasonable budget. Additionally, the approach cannot precompute expected fingerprints without knowing the node-to-position assignment, making it logically incomplete.

For each color c, I'll build a binary lifting table up[u][j] that stores the highest ancestor of u (or -1) that also contains color c, within 2^j steps up the tree. First, I need to preprocess the tree with a DFS to get parent pointers and depths. Then for each color c, I'll do a DFS or BFS to find, for each node u, the nearest ancestor that also has color c — call this anc[u][c]. Then up[u][0] = anc[u][c], and up[u][j] = up[ up[u][j-1] ][j-1] for j>0.

For a query (u, v), I first take the intersection of C_u and C_v — these are the only colors that could possibly appear on the entire path, since both endpoints must have them. Let S = C_u ∩ C_v. For each color c in S, I need to check whether every node on the path from u to v contains c. I can do this by checking two things: first, that the LCA of u and v (in the original tree) also contains color c; second, that the path from u up to LCA consists entirely of nodes containing c, and similarly for v up to LCA. To check the path from u up to LCA, I can use the color-specific lifting table: starting at u, I jump as high as possible while staying at or below LCA and ensuring every ancestor along the jump contains c. If I can reach exactly LCA, then the u-to-LCA segment is fully covered by c. Same for v.

Let me trace this on a concrete small tree. Suppose n=5, edges: 1-2, 2-3, 1-4, 4-5. Colors: node 1 has {1}, node 2 has {1,2}, node 3 has {1}, node 4 has {1}, node 5 has {1,2}. So k=2, s=7. Query: u=3, v=5. Path: 3-2-1-4-5. C_3 = {1}, C_5 = {1,2}. Intersection S = {1}. Now check color 1. LCA of 3 and 5 is node 1, which has color 1. Good. Now check path from 3 up to 1: nodes 3,2,1 all have color 1 — yes. Path from 5 up to 1: nodes 5,4,1 all have color 1 — yes. So answer for this query is 1. Works.

Now another query: u=3, v=5, but let's change node 4 to have no color 1 — say C_4 = {2}. Now path 3-2-1-4-5: node 4 lacks color 1. Intersection S = {1}. LCA=1 has color 1. Check path from 3 to 1: 3,2,1 all have 1 — yes. Path from 5 to 1: 5 has 1, but 4 does not. So the color-specific lifting from 5: anc[5][1] = 1 (since 1 is the nearest ancestor with color 1, skipping 4). Jumping from 5: up[5][0] = 1. LCA is 1. So from 5 I can jump directly to 1 in one step. But wait — this jump skips node 4, which doesn't have color 1. The lifting table only stores ancestors that DO have color 1, so jumping from 5 to 1 via up[5][0] = 1 is valid for the lifting table, but it doesn't tell me that node 4 lacks color 1. The lifting table is built only over nodes that contain color c — it doesn't encode whether intermediate nodes on the original tree path are missing c. So if I jump from 5 to 1, I've "teleported" over node 4, and I have no way to know that node 4 was supposed to be on the path but didn't have color 1. The lifting table only tells me about ancestors that have the color, not about the ones that don't. So the check "can I reach LCA using only color-c nodes" is necessary but not sufficient — I also need to ensure that the original tree path doesn't contain any node missing c. The lifting table alone can't detect gaps.

To detect gaps, I'd need to verify that the distance in the original tree from u to the ancestor reached by the color-specific jump equals the jump distance in the color-specific tree. That is, if I jump from u to some ancestor w using the color-c lifting table, I need to check that depth[u] - depth[w] equals the number of original tree edges between u and w, and that every node on that original path has color c. But the lifting table doesn't store the original tree distances along color-c-only paths — it only stores the ancestors that have color c, not the gaps. So I can't verify that no gaps exist without additional information.

oh wait — the color-specific lifting table only connects nodes that have color c, skipping over nodes that don't. It can't detect whether the original path passes through nodes missing c.

LEARNING: Color-specific lifting skips nodes lacking color c, so a successful jump from u to LCA doesn't guarantee all intermediate original-tree nodes have c — node 4 missing color 1 is invisible to the lifting table.

Alright, back to square one. The per-color bitmasks blew up because they scaled with path length, the segment tree of frequencies was too heavy in k, and the per-color binary lifting missed nodes that lacked the color entirely — the fundamental issue is that checking a color's presence across a path requires verifying every node on that path, which is O(n) per color unless you can somehow pre-aggregate the answer.

Hmm... that phrase from earlier — "transform the color intersection problem into counting shared 'components'" — keeps echoing. What if, for each color, I group all nodes that contain that color and are connected via tree edges? Wait, but the tree edges connect all nodes, regardless of color. That's not right. Oh — but if I consider only the nodes that *have* the color, and connect them if they are adjacent in the original tree, then each color forms a forest of connected components within the tree. Then, for a color to be present in every node along a path from u to v, that entire path must lie within a single component of that color's forest. Because if the path ever left the component, it would cross a node lacking the color, breaking the intersection.

So the problem reduces to: for each query (u,v), count how many colors c such that u and v are in the same component of the forest induced by nodes having color c. That's promising — but checking all k colors per query is still O(k q), too slow. We need to invert the counting. Instead of iterating colors for each query, we can precompute, for each color component, all pairs of nodes within it? That's O(n²) worst case. But the constraints on s give a clue: the total number of (node, color) pairs is only s ≤ 3·10⁵. So the sum over all colors of (size of component) is bounded by s? Not exactly — a node can have multiple colors, so a single node can appear in multiple color components. But the total number of node appearances across all color components is exactly s. That means if I process each color component independently, the total work across all components is O(s). So if I can, for each component, somehow contribute to the answers of all query pairs inside it, without explicitly iterating over all O(size²) pairs, I might get an O(s √s) or O(s log s) solution.

What data structure can answer "for all pairs (u,v) in this set, increment their shared color count" efficiently? That sounds like a DSU rollback or small-to-large merging over queries. Wait — the queries are given separately. I could treat each color component as a set of nodes. For each such set, I need to add +1 to the answer of every query (u,v) where both u and v are in the set. If I pre-process all queries and store, for each node, the queries it participates in, then when I merge nodes into a component, I can check if both endpoints of a query become connected within this color, and if so, increment that query's answer. That's exactly like a DSU that supports query answering offline! For each color, I take all nodes that have that color, and union them along the original tree edges that connect them (but only considering edges where both endpoints have the color). As I union, if two nodes belong to the same DSU component, and there exists a query between them, then for this color, they are in the same component — meaning the color appears on the entire path between them. So I can increment that query's answer.

But I must be careful: I need to process each color independently, resetting the DSU between colors. And I must only union nodes that actually have the color. Also, I must only consider queries where both nodes have the color — otherwise the answer for that color is automatically 0 for that query. So for each color c, I collect all nodes that have c, and all queries (u,v) where both u and v are in that node set. Then I run DSU on the induced subgraph (connecting nodes with original tree edges if both endpoints have c). For each query (u,v) in the set, if find(u) == find(v) after processing all relevant unions, then color c contributes +1 to the answer of that query.

Let me trace a tiny example. Suppose n=4, tree edges: 1-2, 2-3, 3-4. Colors: C1={1}, C2={1,2}, C3={2}, C4={1}. So s=4. Queries: (1,4), (2,3). For color 1: nodes {1,2,4}. Induced edges: 1-2 (both have color 1), 2-3? No, node 3 lacks color 1. 3-4? Node 3 lacks color 1. So components: {1,2} and {4}. Queries involving color 1: (1,4) — nodes 1 and 4 are in different components, so color 1 does NOT contribute to query (1,4). (2,3) — node 3 not in node set for color 1, so ignore. For color 2: nodes {2,3}. Induced edge: 2-3 (both have color 2). Component: {2,3}. Queries: (2,3) — both in same component, so +1 for query (2,3). (1,4) — node 1 not in set, ignore. So final answers: query (1,4)=0, query (2,3)=1. Let's verify manually: Path 1-4 is nodes {1,2,3,4}. Intersection of C1∩C2∩C3∩C4 = {1}∩{1,2}∩{2}∩{1} = {} → 0. Path 2-3: {2,3}, intersection {1,2}∩{2} = {2} → 1. Correct.

But the naive implementation would still be heavy: for each color, I need to build the induced graph and run DSU on its nodes, and also check all queries within that color's node set. The total number of query checks could be large: if a query involves nodes that share many colors, it gets checked many times. But the sum over all colors of (number of queries checked) is at most sum over colors of (number of queries where both endpoints have that color) ≤ q * (max colors per node)? Not directly bounded by s. A node can have up to k colors, but s is small, so average colors per node is s/n ≤ min(k, s). In worst case, a node could have many colors if k is large but s is still bounded? Actually s ≤ nk, but also s ≤ 3·10⁵. So if k is large, each node has on average s/n colors. Still, a query could be checked for up to min(|C_u|, |C_v|) colors, which could be up to s/n each, leading to O(q * s/n) total checks. With n,q,s all up to 3e5, that could be 9e10 in worst case if s/n is large. So we need to limit the number of query checks per color.

Small-to-large merging comes to mind: when processing a color, the DSU unions are done on the induced graph. The number of nodes per color is the frequency of that color. The sum over all colors of frequency_c = s. So if I process each color independently, the total work for DSU operations is O(s α(n)). For queries, I can't afford to check all pairs within the component. Instead, I can store, for each node, the list of queries that involve that node. Then, when uniting two DSU components, I need to check if any query has one endpoint in each component, and if so, and if that query's other endpoint is also in the current color's node set, then this union might make them connected. But I must ensure I only count the query once per color, when the two nodes become connected. So I can use a DSU that, when merging two sets, traverses the smaller query list to check for matches in the larger set. This is classic offline query solving with DSU and small-to-large merging on query lists. The total work becomes O((s + q) log n) because each query is moved O(log n) times.

Let's trace this refined version on the same example. Preprocess queries: for node 1, queries: (1,4). Node 2: queries: (1,4), (2,3). Node 3: queries: (2,3). Node 4: queries: (1,4). Now process color 1: nodes {1,2,4}. Initialize DSU with each node alone. Query lists attached to DSU leaders: leader 1: {(1,4, id0)}, leader 2: {(1,4, id0), (2,3, id1)}, leader 4: {(1,4, id0)}. Induced edges: (1,2) both have color 1. Merge components of 1 and 2. Smaller query list is leader 1's (size 1) vs leader 2's (size 2). Check each query in smaller list: query (1,4, id0). The other endpoint is 4. Find leader of 4: currently 4. Since 4 is not in the component being merged into (leader 2), we don't have both endpoints in the same component yet. So we just merge the query lists: new leader 2 has list {(1,4, id0), (1,4, id0), (2,3, id1)} but we need to deduplicate or store counts. Actually we can store for each query id the set of nodes that have it; easier: when merging, if a query appears in both components, then both endpoints are now in the same component, so we can increment answer for that query id. Here, query (1,4) appears in both component 1 and component 4? Wait, component 4 is still separate. So after merging 1 and 2, query (1,4) appears twice in the combined component (from nodes 1 and 2), but the other endpoint 4 is still outside. So no increment. Next induced edge: none other connect nodes with color 1. So processing for color 1 ends without incrementing any query. Now process color 2: nodes {2,3}. DSU reset. Leaders: 2 with queries {(1,4, id0), (2,3, id1)}; 3 with queries {(2,3, id1)}. Induced edge: (2,3). Merge components 2 and 3. Smaller list is leader 3's (size 1). Check query (2,3, id1). Other endpoint is 2, whose leader is 2 (the other component). Since we are merging these two components, after merge they will be together, so this query now has both endpoints in the same component. So we increment answer[id1] by 1. Then merge query lists. End. Final answers: id0=0, id1=1. Correct.

The subtlety: we must ensure we only count a query for a color when both endpoints have that color. In the merging step, we checked that the other endpoint is in the current color's node set implicitly because we only process nodes that have the color, and the query lists only contain queries where the node is involved — but the other endpoint might not have the color. In the merge for color 2, query (2,3) both endpoints have color 2, so it's valid. For color 1, query (1,4) both endpoints have color 1, but they never ended up in same component, so no increment. That works.

But wait — what about queries where the two nodes have the color but the path between them includes nodes without the color? In that case, in the induced graph (only nodes with color c), those intermediate nodes are missing, so the two nodes will be in different components, and the merge will never unite them. So the query won't get incremented. That's correct.

So the algorithm: for each color c, collect all nodes that have c. Build the induced graph by iterating over those nodes and for each, look at its adjacency list in the original tree, and if the neighbor also has color c, add an edge between them (but be careful to add each edge once). Then run DSU on this induced graph, merging components while processing queries with small-to-large on query lists attached to DSU components. For each merge, process the smaller query list: for each query id in it, if the other endpoint of the query is in the other component (i.e., its leader is the other component's leader before merge), then increment answer[id] by 1 (because this color now contributes). Then merge the query lists.

Complexities: total nodes across all color graphs is s. Total edges across all induced graphs? Each original edge (u,v) will be considered for each color that both u and v have. So total edge checks could be O(s * deg) in worst case? But we can limit: for each color, we iterate over nodes having that color, and for each such node, iterate over its neighbors. So total work is sum over colors of (sum over nodes with color c of deg(node)). That's sum over nodes of (deg(node) * |C_u|). Since sum |C_u| = s, and sum deg = 2(n-1), the total is O(s * deg_avg). In worst case, a node could have degree O(n) and many colors, but s is bounded, so deg_avg is O(1) because sum deg = O(n). Actually, the worst term is sum over nodes of (deg * |C|) ≤ max_deg * s. max_deg could be O(n) in a star, so that's O(n s) which is too big. We need a better way to build induced edges. Instead, for each original edge (u,v), we can consider the intersection of C_u and C_v. For each color c in the intersection, this edge connects u and v in color c's induced graph. So we can, for each edge, iterate over the common colors (smaller set of the two) and add that edge to the list for that color. The total work is sum over edges of min(|C_u|, |C_v|). This is bounded by s * something? Not directly, but in worst case, if every node has many colors, s is large, but s is bounded by 3e5. So sum over edges of min(|C_u|,|C_v|) ≤ sum over edges of (|C_u|+|C_v|)/2 = (1/2) * sum over edges of (|C_u|+|C_v|) = (1/2) * (sum over nodes of deg(node)*|C_u|). Same as before. Still could be O(n s). But note: s ≤ 3e5, n ≤ 3e5, so n*s could be 9e10. That's unacceptable. We need to avoid iterating over all edges for all colors they share. Wait — the total number of (node, color) pairs is s. Each edge (u,v) will be processed for each color in C_u ∩ C_v. The size of C_u ∩ C_v is at most min(|C_u|,|C_v|). The sum over all edges of min(|C_u|,|C_v|) is actually bounded by something like: consider each node u, for each color c in C_u, how many neighbors v also have color c? That's at most deg(u). So total contributions = sum over u of sum over c in C_u of (number of neighbors with c). That's at most sum over u of |C_u| * deg(u) ≤ max_deg * s. In worst case max_deg = n-1, so O(n s). <code>
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define fastio ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) (x).rbegin(),(x).rend()
#define sz(x) (int)(x).size()
#define ll long long
#define pb push_back
#define pob pop_back
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pll pair<ll , ll>
#define vpll vector<pll>
#define endl '\n'
#define MAX(a) *max_element(all(a))
#define MIN(a) *min_element(all(a))

ll gcd(ll a, ll b) {
    while (a > 0) {
        ll temp = b % a;
        b = a;
        a = temp;
    }
    return b;
}

ll lcm(ll a, ll b) {
    return a / gcd(a, b) * b;
}

ll ceildiv(ll n, ll k) {
    ll t = n / k;
    if (n % k == 0) return t;
    return t + 1;
}

ll pw(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r *= a;
        a *= a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

ll modpw(ll a, ll b, ll mod) {
    ll r = 1 % mod;
    a %= mod;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) r = r * a % mod;
        a = a * a % mod;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return r;
}

const ll MAXN = 3E5+16, B = 800;
vll adj[MAXN];
vll cols[MAXN], comps[MAXN];
ll comp_col[MAXN];
ll timer = 1, cur = 1;
ll last[MAXN];
bool active[MAXN];
ll curMap[MAXN];
int preProc[MAXN/B+16][MAXN];

void dfs1(ll u, ll par) {
    vll th(cols[u].size()), th2(cols[u].size());
    comps[u].resize(cols[u].size());
    for (ll i = 0; i < (ll)cols[u].size(); i++) {
        ll c = cols[u][i];
        th[i] = last[c];
        th2[i] = comp_col[c];
        comp_col[c] = (last[c] == par ? comp_col[c] : timer++);
        comps[u][i] = comp_col[c];
        last[c] = u;
    }
    for (ll v : adj[u]) {
        if (v == par) continue;
        dfs1(v, u);
    }
    for (ll i = 0; i < (ll)cols[u].size(); i++) {
        ll c = cols[u][i];
        last[c] = th[i];
        comp_col[c] = th2[i];
    }
}

void solve() {
    ll n, k, s, q;
    cin >> n >> k >> s >> q;
    fill(adj, adj+n, vll({}));
    for (ll i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        ll u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;
        u--; v--;
        adj[u].pb(v);
        adj[v].pb(u);
    }
    timer = 1;
    cur = 1;
    fill(cols, cols+n, vll({}));
    fill(comps, comps+n, vll({}));
    fill(comp_col, comp_col+k, -16);
    fill(last, last+k, -16);
    for (ll i = 0; i < s; i++) {
        ll x, c;
        cin >> x >> c;
        x--; c--;
        cols[x].pb(c);
    }
    dfs1(0, 0);
    for (ll u = 0; u < n; u++) {
        sort(comps[u].begin(), comps[u].end());
    }
    for (ll u = 0; u < n; u++) {
        if ((ll)cols[u].size() < B) continue;
        for (ll c : comps[u]) active[c] = true;
        for (ll v = 0; v < n; v++) {
            preProc[cur][v] = 0;
            for (ll c : comps[v]) {
                preProc[cur][v] += active[c];
            }
        }
        for (ll c : comps[u]) active[c] = false;
        curMap[u] = cur++;
    }
    while (q--) {
        ll u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;
        u--; v--;
        if ((ll)cols[u].size() >= B) {
            cout << preProc[curMap[u]][v] << ' ';
            continue;
        }
        if ((ll)cols[v].size() >= B) {
            cout << preProc[curMap[v]][u] << ' ';
            continue;
        }
        ll ans = 0;
        ll p1 = 0;
        for (ll p2 = 0; p2 < (ll)comps[v].size(); p2++) {
            while (p1 < (ll)comps[u].size() && comps[u][p1] < comps[v][p2]) p1++;
            if (p1 == (ll)comps[u].size() || comps[u][p1] != comps[v][p2]) continue;
            ans++;
        }
        cout << ans << ' ';
    }
    cout << '\n';
}

int main() {
    cin.tie(nullptr)->sync_with_stdio(false);
    ll T; cin >> T; while (T--) { solve(); }
    return 0;
}

So the correct check: for each segment of length len, the segment tree returns sum_freq[c]. If sum_freq[c] == len for that segment, then color c is present in all nodes of that segment. At the end, count colors still true. But iterating over k colors per segment gives O(k log n) per query. With k up to 3×10^5 and q up to 3×10^5, that's 9×10^10 operations, same order as the bitset approach already rejected.

The pruning idea — skip rectangles where area exceeds best answer — doesn't help because the bottleneck is per-query processing of k colors across O(log n) segments, not the enumeration of rectangles.

The fundamental issue: any approach that iterates over all k colors per query will be O(k log n) per query, which with k,q ≤ 3×10^5 is ~10^10–10^11 operations, far beyond budget. The problem requires a way to avoid scanning all colors per query, perhaps by only considering colors that actually appear on the path, or using some meet-in-the-middle or DSU technique as suggested by the tags.

LEARNING: The segment-tree-of-frequency-arrays approach requires O(k log n) per query to check all colors across segments; with k,q ≤ 3·10^5 this is ~10^10 operations, exceeding any reasonable budget. Additionally, the approach cannot precompute expected fingerprints without knowing the node-to-position assignment, making it logically incomplete.

For each color c, I'll build a binary lifting table up[u][j] that stores the highest ancestor of u (or -1) that also contains color c, within 2^j steps up the tree. First, I need to preprocess the tree with a DFS to get parent pointers and depths. Then for each color c, I'll do a DFS or BFS to find, for each node u, the nearest ancestor that also has color c — call this anc[u][c]. Then up[u][0] = anc[u][c], and up[u][j] = up[ up[u][j-1] ][j-1] for j>0.

For a query (u, v), I first take the intersection of C_u and C_v — these are the only colors that could possibly appear on the entire path, since both endpoints must have them. Let S = C_u ∩ C_v. For each color c in S, I need to check whether every node on the path from u to v contains c. I can do this by checking two things: first, that the LCA of u and v (in the original tree) also contains color c; second, that the path from u up to LCA consists entirely of nodes containing c, and similarly for v up to LCA. To check the path from u up to LCA, I can use the color-specific lifting table: starting at u, I jump as high as possible while staying at or below LCA and ensuring every ancestor along the jump contains c. If I can reach exactly LCA, then the u-to-LCA segment is fully covered by c. Same for v.

Let me trace this on a concrete small tree. Suppose n=5, edges: 1-2, 2-3, 1-4, 4-5. Colors: node 1 has {1}, node 2 has {1,2}, node 3 has {1}, node 4 has {1}, node 5 has {1,2}. So k=2, s=7. Query: u=3, v=5. Path: 3-2-1-4-5. C_3 = {1}, C_5 = {1,2}. Intersection S = {1}. Now check color 1. LCA of 3 and 5 is node 1, which has color 1. Good. Now check path from 3 up to 1: nodes 3,2,1 all have color 1 — yes. Path from 5 up to 1: nodes 5,4,1 all have color 1 — yes. So answer for this query is 1. Works.

Now another query: u=3, v=5, but let's change node 4 to have no color 1 — say C_4 = {2}. Now path 3-2-1-4-5: node 4 lacks color 1. Intersection S = {1}. LCA=1 has color 1. Check path from 3 to 1: 3,2,1 all have 1 — yes. Path from 5 to 1: 5 has 1, but 4 does not. So the color-specific lifting from 5: anc[5][1] = 1 (since 1 is the nearest ancestor with color 1, skipping 4). Jumping from 5: up[5][0] = 1. LCA is 1. So from 5 I can jump directly to 1 in one step. But wait — this jump skips node 4, which doesn't have color 1. The lifting table only stores ancestors that DO have color 1, so jumping from 5 to 1 via up[5][0] = 1 is valid for the lifting table, but it doesn't tell me that node 4 lacks color 1. The lifting table is built only over nodes that contain color c — it doesn't encode whether intermediate nodes on the original tree path are missing c. So if I jump from 5 to 1, I've "teleported" over node 4, and I have no way to know that node 4 was supposed to be on the path but didn't have color 1. The lifting table only tells me about ancestors that have the color, not about the ones that don't. So the check "can I reach LCA using only color-c nodes" is necessary but not sufficient — I also need to ensure that the original tree path doesn't contain any node missing c. The lifting table alone can't detect gaps.

To detect gaps, I'd need to verify that the distance in the original tree from u to the ancestor reached by the color-specific jump equals the jump distance in the color-specific tree. That is, if I jump from u to some ancestor w using the color-c lifting table, I need to check that depth[u] - depth[w] equals the number of original tree edges between u and w, and that every node on that original path has color c. But the lifting table doesn't store the original tree distances along color-c-only paths — it only stores the ancestors that have color c, not the gaps. So I can't verify that no gaps exist without additional information.

oh wait — the color-specific lifting table only connects nodes that have color c, skipping over nodes that don't. It can't detect whether the original path passes through nodes missing c.

LEARNING: Color-specific lifting skips nodes lacking color c, so a successful jump from u to LCA doesn't guarantee all intermediate original-tree nodes have c — node 4 missing color 1 is invisible to the lifting table.

That's O(|V_c|). So for each color, mark nodes in a hash set or boolean array of size n? But n can be 3e5, and we do this per color, total O(s + n * number_of_colors) which could be large. Instead, we can sort V_c and use binary search to check membership. That's O(|V_c| log |V_c|) per color, total O(s log s). Acceptable.

Then, for each induced edge (u, parent[u]) where both in V_c, union them in DSU. During union, merge the query maps as described. At the end, output answers.

aha! — the core insight is that a color contributes to a query iff the two query nodes are connected in the induced subgraph of nodes having that color, which turns the problem into offline DSU with query answering per color, leveraging the sparsity of color occurrences (s) to keep total work near-linear. The parent-edge trick reduces induced graph construction to O(s), and small-to-large merging on query maps keeps the query processing efficient. The earlier failed approaches all tried to answer queries online or per query, which forced checking all colors or all nodes on the path. This approach inverts it: process each color independently, using DSU to test connectivity of query nodes within the color's component, which automatically ensures the color appears on every node along the original path because the induced graph connectivity mirrors the original path's continuity for that color.

finetuned.full: To solve this problem efficiently, we need to process up to \(3 \times 10^5\) queries on a tree with up to \(3 \times 10^5\) nodes. This means we cannot afford quadratic operations per query. We'll leverage depth-first search (DFS) with memoization and bit manipulation. Firstly, note that the total number of edges \(m = n - 1\). We can represent the tree using adjacency lists. For each node, we keep track of the set of colors \(C_u\) as a bitmask (since \(k \leq 3 \times 10^5\)). We will perform DFS from the root to compute the bitmask for each subtree. Specifically, for each node \(u\), we maintain a bitmask representing the union of colors seen along the path from the root to \(u\). When processing a child \(v\), we update the bitmask by taking the intersection of the current path bitmask and the bitmask of \(C_v\). This way, when answering a query between two nodes \(u\) and \(v\), we simply take the bitwise AND of their respective path bitmasks and count the number of set bits in the result. This gives us the size of the intersection of the color sets along the unique path between \(u\) and \(v\). Let's outline the steps: 1. Parse the input and build the tree. 2. Perform DFS from the root to compute the bitmask for each node. 3. Answer each query by computing the bitwise AND of the two node masks and counting the set bits. Complexity analysis: - Building the tree takes \(O(n)\). - DFS runs in \(O(n + q)\) because we visit each node once and handle each query once. - Counting set bits can be done in constant time using built-in functions. Constraints are handled well within the \(3 \times 10^5\) limit. #include using namespace std; #define forn(i, n) for (int i = 0; i < int(n); ++i) #define trav(a, x) for (auto& a : x) const int MAX_K = 3e5; const int MAX_N = 3e5; vector adj[MAX_N]; int mask[MAX_N]; void dfs(int u, int p, const vector>& colors) { mask[u] = 0; for (int x : colors[u]) mask[u] |= 1 << x; for (int v : adj[u]) { if (v == p) continue; dfs(v, u, colors); mask[u] &= mask[v]; } } vector solve(const vector>& edges, const vector>& colors, const vector>& queries) { int n = (int)adj[0].size(); forn(u, n) adj[u].clear(); forn(e, n - 1) { auto [u,v] = edges[e]; --u, --v; adj[u].push_back(v); adj[v].push_back(u); } forn(u, n) mask[u] = 0; dfs(0, -1, colors); vector ans; for (auto [u,v] : queries) { --u, --v; ans.push_back(__builtin_popcount(mask[u] & mask[v])); } return ans; } int main() { ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) { int n, k, s, q; cin >> n >> k >> s >> q; vector> edges(n - 1); vector> colors(n + 1); forn(i, n - 1) { auto [u, v] = edges[i]; cin >> u >> v; } forn(i, s) { int v, x; cin >> v >> x; --v; colors[v].push_back(x); } vector> queries(q); forn(i, q) { auto [u, v] = queries[i]; cin >> u >> v; } auto ans = solve(edges, colors, queries); for (int x : ans) cout << x << " "; cout << "\n"; } return 0; }

To solve this problem efficiently, we need to process up to \(3 \times 10^5\) queries on a tree with up to \(3 \times 10^5\) nodes. This means we cannot afford quadratic operations per query. We'll leverage depth-first search (DFS) with memoization and bit manipulation.

Firstly, note that the total number of edges \(m = n - 1\). We can represent the tree using adjacency lists. For each node, we keep track of the set of colors \(C_u\) as a bitmask (since \(k \leq 3 \times 10^5\)).

We will perform DFS from the root to compute the bitmask for each subtree. Specifically, for each node \(u\), we maintain a bitmask representing the union of colors seen along the path from the root to \(u\). When processing a child \(v\), we update the bitmask by taking the intersection of the current path bitmask and the bitmask of \(C_v\).

This way, when answering a query between two nodes \(u\) and \(v\), we simply take the bitwise AND of their respective path bitmasks and count the number of set bits in the result. This gives us the size of the intersection of the color sets along the unique path between \(u\) and \(v\).

Let's outline the steps:

1. Parse the input and build the tree.
2. Perform DFS from the root to compute the bitmask for each node.
3. Answer each query by computing the bitwise AND of the two node masks and counting the set bits.

Complexity analysis:
- Building the tree takes \(O(n)\).
- DFS runs in \(O(n + q)\) because we visit each node once and handle each query once.
- Counting set bits can be done in constant time using built-in functions.

Constraints are handled well within the \(3 \times 10^5\) limit.

problem:
[PROBLEM]

[STATEMENT]
You are given a weighted directed graph with $$$n$$$ vertices and $$$m$$$ edges. Each vertex in the graph can be either highlighted or normal. Initially, all vertices are normal. The cost of the graph is defined as the minimum sum of edge weights that need to be selected so that from each normal vertex one can reach at least one highlighted vertex using the selected edges only. If it is not possible to select the edges, the cost is $$$-1$$$ instead.

Your task is to compute the cost of the graph after each of the $$$q$$$ queries. The queries can be of two types:

- $$$+\;v_i$$$ makes vertex $$$v_i$$$ highlighted; it is guaranteed that the vertex is normal before the query.
- $$$-\;v_i$$$ makes vertex $$$v_i$$$ normal; it is guaranteed that the vertex is highlighted before the query.

Output the cost of the graph after each of the $$$q$$$ queries.

Time limit: 2s
Memory limit: 512MB

Input:
The first line contains three integers $$$n$$$, $$$m$$$, and $$$q$$$ ($$$3 \le n \le 2 \cdot 10^5, 1 \le m, q \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$) — the number of vertices in the graph, the number of edges, and the number of queries.

The next $$$m$$$ lines contain the edges of the graph, one edge per line. The $$$i$$$-th line contains three integers $$$u_i$$$, $$$v_i$$$, and $$$c_i$$$ ($$$1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq n, u_i \ne v_i, 1 \leq c_i \leq 10^6$$$) — the endpoints of the $$$i$$$-th edge (from $$$u_i$$$ to $$$v_i$$$) and its weight ($$$c_i$$$).

The next $$$q$$$ lines contain the queries, one query per line. The $$$i$$$-th line contains $$$+\;v_i$$$, if it is a query of the first type, and $$$-\;v_i$$$, if it is a query of the second type ($$$1 \leq v_i \leq n$$$).

Output:
Output $$$q$$$ numbers. The $$$i$$$-th number is the cost of the graph after the first $$$i$$$ queries.

Note:
In the first test:

- After the first query, it is most profitable to select the edges with numbers $$$3, 4, 5$$$, their total cost is $$$15$$$.
- After the second query, there are no highlighted vertices, which means that there are no suitable sets of edges, the cost of the graph is $$$-1$$$.
- After the third query, it is most profitable to select the edges with numbers $$$1, 2, 5$$$, their total cost is $$$14$$$.
- After the fourth query, it is most profitable to select the edges with numbers $$$4$$$ and $$$5$$$, their total cost is $$$12$$$.
- After the fifth query, it is most profitable to select only edge number $$$5$$$, its cost is $$$4$$$.
- After the sixth query, all vertices are highlighted and there is no need to select any edges, the cost of the graph is $$$0$$$.
Examples:
  Example 1:
    Input:
    4 5 6
    1 2 1
    2 3 5
    3 2 3
    4 1 8
    2 1 4
    + 1
    - 1
    + 3
    + 1
    + 4
    + 2
    Output:
    15
    -1
    14
    12
    4
    0

  Example 2:
    Input:
    10 14 10
    8 6 4
    2 5 1
    3 5 4
    1 6 3
    1 3 7
    7 2 1
    6 1 3
    4 10 1
    4 6 5
    5 4 1
    5 8 10
    10 9 1
    9 5 1
    9 7 6
    + 7
    + 8
    - 7
    + 10
    + 2
    - 10
    + 5
    - 2
    - 5
    + 3
    Output:
    28
    24
    29
    19
    18
    24
    18
    19
    29
    20

So the approach works on Example 1. Let me check complexity. Each query: run multi-source Dijkstra on reverse graph. n ≤ 2×10^5, m ≤ 2×10^5. wait — min-cost flow charges c per unit of flow, but we need to pay c once per selected edge regardless of how many paths use it. The cost structure is a fixed charge, not a per-unit cost.

LEARNING: Min-cost flow charges per unit traversing each edge (k·c for k paths), but the problem pays c once per selected edge regardless of usage count — flow overcounts by factor equal to edge sharing.

Right, so nothing worked so far. The full Dijkstra per query is astronomically slow, and the flow model fundamentally misrepresents the cost structure because we pay for an edge once even if it serves multiple paths.

hmm. That nagging thought about reversing the graph and a static contraction tree... wait — if we reverse all edges, then the condition "from each normal vertex one can reach a highlighted vertex" becomes "from each highlighted vertex, can be reached by all normal vertices via the selected edges". The selected edges now form a structure that reaches *into* the highlighted set from the rest of the graph. In the reversed graph, we need to ensure every normal vertex has a *path to* a highlighted vertex. That's equivalent to selecting edges so that every normal vertex is in the "in-arborescence" rooted at some highlighted vertex. The cost is the minimum total weight of edges to connect all normal vertices to some highlighted root.

Oh! This is exactly the *minimum spanning arborescence in a directed graph* problem — but with *multiple roots* (the highlighted vertices). That's the classic *minimum spanning forest in a directed graph* problem, which can be solved by adding a super-source connected to all highlighted vertices with zero-weight edges, then finding the minimum arborescence rooted at that super-source. The answer is the total weight of that arborescence minus the zero-weight edges we added. But we can't recompute the entire arborescence from scratch every query.

But there's a static contraction algorithm for arborescences — Chu–Liu/Edmonds' algorithm. It contracts cycles, building a hierarchy. The final answer is the sum of the minimum incoming edge weight for each node (except roots), minus adjustments for cycles. If we precompute this contraction DAG on the *static graph*, the contribution of each node/contracted component to the total cost is fixed, *unless* that component contains a highlighted vertex. Because if a component contains a highlighted vertex, then all nodes inside it are already "covered" — we don't need to pay for the edge that enters that component from outside. So the answer becomes the sum of the minimum incoming edge weights for all components that do *not* contain a highlighted vertex.

Let me derive the data structure. Run Chu–Liu/Edmonds on the reversed static graph. It produces a forest of contraction "meta-nodes". Each meta-node has a "incoming edge" that is the cheapest edge entering it from outside during the contraction. The algorithm builds a DAG where each meta-node points to its parent via that chosen edge. The total weight of the minimum arborescence rooted at a hypothetical super-root is the sum of these chosen edge weights over all meta-nodes. Now, if a meta-node contains *any* highlighted vertex, then in the multiple-root setting, we don't need to pay for its incoming edge — we can root that component at the highlighted vertex inside it for free. So the answer is the sum of chosen edge weights for all meta-nodes that are *highlight-free*.

We need to support dynamic updates: highlighting/unhighlighting a vertex. That changes which meta-nodes are "covered". When a vertex becomes highlighted, all meta-nodes on its path to the root of the contraction DAG become covered. Their incoming edges no longer count toward the total. When a vertex becomes normal, we must add back the weights of meta-nodes on its path that become uncovered (i.e., no other highlighted vertex in that meta-node's subtree).

So we need to maintain, for each meta-node, whether its subtree contains any highlighted vertex. The total cost is the sum of weights of meta-nodes whose "contains-highlight" flag is false. When we toggle a vertex, we walk up the DAG from its leaf meta-node, flipping flags, and adjusting the sum whenever a meta-node's flag changes from false→true (subtract its weight) or true→false (add its weight).

But wait — the contraction DAG is a tree? In Chu–Liu/Edmonds, after full contraction, the chosen edges form an arborescence rooted at the super-root.
